NHEJ is the template-free pathway that directly rejoins the two ends of a single break. The stem specifies strand invasion and a Holliday junction, which is homologous recombination. NHEJ never performs homology-guided arm exchange between homologs.

SDSA is a **non-crossover** pathway: the invading strand is displaced and re-anneals to its own broken end, so flanking arms are never exchanged. More decisively, the sister chromatid is genetically identical, so copying from it could never turn an *A B* chromatid into *A b*.
questions.src: L5 — 'I do need you to recognise the name Holliday junction and recognise the phrase opposite-sense resolution.' Cutting mechanics deliberately not required.

questions.stem: You have isolated a bacterial strain with the genotype **his⁻ leu⁺ lac⁺ gal⁻ strR ampS**. You want to grow it. Which single plate below will support growth of this strain?
options: Minimal medium plus galactose as the sole carbon source, plus histidine, plus streptomycin.
Minimal medium plus lactose as the sole carbon source, plus leucine, plus ampicillin.
Minimal medium plus lactose as the sole carbon source, plus histidine, plus streptomycin.
Minimal medium plus galactose as the sole carbon source, plus histidine and leucine, with no antibiotic.
Minimal medium plus lactose as the sole carbon source, plus histidine, plus ampicillin.
questions.why: Work the four classes separately. **his⁻** means the strain cannot make histidine, so histidine **must be supplied**. **lac⁺** means it can break lactose down, so lactose **works** as the carbon source. **gal⁻** means it cannot use galactose, so galactose as the sole carbon source starves it. **strR** means streptomycin does not kill it. Only one plate does all three at once: supplies histidine, offers a sugar the strain can actually catabolise, and uses an antibiotic it resists.
wrong: Galactose fails. **gal⁻** means the strain cannot break galactose down, so as the sole carbon source it provides nothing — supplying a sugar only helps if the strain can catabolise it.

Two failures. Histidine is absent and the strain is **his⁻**, so it cannot make its own. Leucine is supplied but the strain is **leu⁺** and never needed it. Ampicillin also kills it, since it is **ampS**.
Galactose again fails as the sole carbon source for a **gal⁻** strain. The absence of antibiotic is fine and the amino acids are adequate, but with no usable carbon source there is no growth.
The carbon source and histidine are both correct here, but the strain is **ampS** — sensitive to ampicillin — so the antibiotic kills it.
questions.worked:
Split the genotype by class before touching the options.

**Amino acids (biosynthesis, anabolic):** his⁻ = broken, so histidine **must be added**. leu⁺ = works, so leucine is **not** needed (adding it is harmless but pointless).

**Sugars (metabolism, catabolic):** lac⁺ = works, so the strain **can grow on** lactose. gal⁻ = broken, so galactose is **useless** to it.

**Antibiotics:** strR = survives streptomycin. ampS = killed by ampicillin.

Notice the trap: a **⁻** amino acid gene means *supply it*, but a **⁻** sugar gene means *supplying it does not help*. Same symbol, opposite instruction, because building a molecule and eating one are opposite processes.

Now screen the options — you need (1) histidine present, (2) lactose not galactose, (3) streptomycin or nothing, never ampicillin. Only B satisfies all three.
questions.src: L6 — the ± / R-S convention she flagged as the thing that makes the homework unreadable if you miss it.

questions.stem: Two phages are used to move genes between bacterial strains. Phage **Alpha** can transfer essentially any gene from the donor chromosome, each at low and roughly equal frequency. Phage **Beta** transfers only the two genes lying immediately adjacent to a single fixed chromosomal location, and does so at high frequency. Which statement BEST accounts for the difference?
options: Alpha performs generalized transduction, mispackaging random host fragments during the lytic cycle; Beta performs specialized transduction, carrying flanking genes after imprecise excision of an integrated prophage.
Alpha performs specialized transduction from a lysogenic prophage; Beta performs generalized transduction during the lytic cycle.
Alpha is a temperate phage and Beta is virulent, which is what determines the range of genes each can carry.
Alpha transfers genes by conjugation and Beta by transformation, which explains the difference in specificity.
Both are generalized transducers; Beta simply infects a strain in which only two genes differ between donor and recipient.
questions.why: **Generalized transduction** happens in the **lytic** cycle: as phage heads are filled, a random fragment of degraded host chromosome is occasionally packaged by mistake, so **any** gene can be moved, each rarely. **Specialized transduction** requires a **lysogenic** prophage integrated at a specific *att* site; when excision is imprecise it takes the **flanking** host genes with it, so only genes near that one site move. Aberrant excision is itself **rare** — the high frequency is *per marker*: every aberrant excision necessarily captures those same two flanking genes, whereas generalized transduction has to happen to package one particular gene out of the entire chromosome.
wrong: This has the two backwards. Random-any-gene is the signature of generalized transduction, and fixed-location-flanking-genes is the signature of specialized transduction.

Beta must be temperate, since specialized transduction requires a prophage integrated in the chromosome, and a strictly virulent phage never integrates. The temperate/virulent distinction is relevant but this option assigns it the wrong way round and treats it as the whole explanation.

Neither process is conjugation or transformation. Both are transduction, meaning phage-mediated transfer. Conjugation needs cell-to-cell contact through a pilus, and transformation is uptake of naked DNA.
The donor and recipient genotypes do not determine which genes a phage is physically able to package. Beta's restriction to two genes reflects where its prophage sits in the chromosome.
questions.src: L6 — transduction, part of the Jul 14 session she confirmed is on Exam I.

questions.stem:
Using a transducing phage grown on a **thr⁺ ile⁺ nad⁺** donor, you infect a **thr⁻ ile⁻ nad⁻** recipient and select for each wild-type marker in turn. The unselected markers are scored in each experiment.

| Experiment I (select thr⁺) | Experiment II (select ile⁺) | Experiment III (select nad⁺) |
|---|---|---|
| ile⁻ nad⁻ 82% | thr⁻ nad⁻ 41% | thr⁻ ile⁻ 18% |
| ile⁺ nad⁻ 0% | thr⁺ nad⁻ 0% | thr⁺ ile⁻ 12% |
| ile⁻ nad⁺ 14% | thr⁻ nad⁺ 57% | thr⁻ ile⁺ 64% |
| ile⁺ nad⁺ 4% | thr⁺ nad⁺ 2% | thr⁺ ile⁺ 6% |

What is the gene order?
options: thr – ile – nad
The data are insufficient to determine an order.
ile – thr – nad
nad – thr – ile
thr – nad – ile
questions.why: Compute the three pairwise cotransduction frequencies. **thr-ile** is by far the lowest (0 + 4 = 4% from I; 0 + 2 = 2% from II), so thr and ile are the **outermost** pair and **nad** must lie between them. The two 0% classes clinch it: those are exactly the thr⁺ile⁺nad⁻ combinations, which carry both outer markers **without** the middle one and therefore require **four** crossovers.
wrong: This puts ile in the middle, but ile-thr is the *least* frequent pair, meaning they are farthest apart. A middle gene must be cotransduced frequently with **both** flanking genes, and ile is cotransduced with thr only 2-4% of the time.
The data are ample and internally consistent. Three pairwise frequencies plus the zero classes over-determine the answer.
This puts thr in the middle, but thr-ile at 2-4% is the weakest linkage in the set, so thr cannot be adjacent to ile at all — let alone between it and nad.
This places thr between nad and ile, which again requires thr-ile to be a close pair. It is the most distant pair.
questions.worked:
**Step 1 — read the frequencies.** Cotransduction frequency for a pair is the sum of **every** class in which both are wild-type... more precisely, for the selected marker X, the cotransduction of X with Y is the sum of all rows where Y is ⁺.

- **thr-nad**, from Experiment I (selecting thr⁺): rows where nad is ⁺ = 14 + 4 = **18%**. Cross-check from Experiment III (selecting nad⁺): rows where thr is ⁺ = 12 + 6 = **18%**. The two estimates agree.
- **ile-nad**, from Experiment II (selecting ile⁺): 57 + 2 = **59%**. Cross-check from III: 64 + 6 = **70%**. Same ballpark, comfortably the highest.
- **thr-ile**, from Experiment I: 0 + 4 = **4%**. Cross-check from II: 0 + 2 = **2%**. By far the lowest.

**Step 2 — rank them.** Cotransduction frequency is **inversely** related to distance: the more often two markers travel together on one phage fragment, the closer they are.

`ile-nad (59-70%)  >  thr-nad (18%)  >>  thr-ile (2-4%)`

**Step 3 — the lowest pair are the outer genes.** thr and ile are hardest to carry together, so they sit at the two ends, and **nad is in the middle**:

**thr – nad – ile**

**Step 4 — confirm independently with the zero class.** Look at the 0% rows: `ile⁺nad⁻` in Experiment I means thr⁺ile⁺nad⁻, and `thr⁺nad⁻` in Experiment II means the same genotype. That class carries **both outer markers but not the middle one**, which cannot be produced by two crossovers — it needs **four**. Four crossovers are vanishingly rare, hence 0%. That the observed zero falls exactly where the four-crossover class should be is strong, independent confirmation of the order.

**Step 5 — sanity check the middle gene's two distances.** nad is closer to ile (59-70%) than to thr (18%), so the map is asymmetric: thr — long gap — nad — short gap — ile.
questions.src: L6 — cotransduction mapping, and the four-crossover logic she teaches as the rigorous confirmation of gene order.

questions.stem:
You are mapping the circular chromosome of a newly isolated *Enterobacter* strain. You have four independent Hfr derivatives, each carrying the F factor integrated at a different site. Each Hfr is mated to an F⁻ recipient and the mating is interrupted by blending at intervals. All four donors are streptomycin-sensitive and the recipient is resistant, so streptomycin in the plates counter-selects the donors; *str* is therefore **not** one of the mapped markers. The table gives the time in minutes at which each mapped marker first appears among recombinants. A dash means the marker never appeared before mating was stopped at 60 minutes.

| Order of entry | Hfr1 | Hfr2 | Hfr3 | Hfr4 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1st | thr (5) | gal (3) | pro (8) | lac (4) |
| 2nd | leu (17) | his (13) | leu (26) | gal (24) |
| 3rd | pro (35) | mal (30) | thr (38) | his (34) |
| 4th | lac (50) | thr (38) | mal (46) | mal (51) |
| 5th | — | leu (50) | — | thr (59) |

**(a)** Determine the complete circular gene order.
**(b)** Give the distance in minutes between each pair of adjacent genes.
**(c)** One of these four strains transfers its chromosome in the opposite direction from the other three. Identify it and explain how the data reveal it.
questions.answer:
**Circular order:** thr – leu – pro – lac – gal – his – mal – (back to thr).

**Intervals:** thr–leu 12, leu–pro 18, pro–lac 15, lac–gal 20, gal–his 10, his–mal 17, mal–thr 8. (Total 100 minutes — but see the worked solution: that total is *not* a check on your work.)

**Hfr3 transfers in the opposite direction.** Its gene run pro → leu → thr is the exact reverse of the thr → leu → pro order Hfr1 shows, and it also runs against the mal → thr direction shared by Hfr2 and Hfr4 — so three strains outvote it.

On a circle, thr–leu–pro–lac–gal–his–mal and its mirror image mal–his–gal–lac–pro–leu–thr are the same map. Both are correct unless the question fixes a reference point.
questions.worked:
**Step 1 — turn each strain's times into an ordered strip.** Drop the numbers for a moment and keep only the sequence:

- Hfr1: thr – leu – pro – lac
- Hfr2: gal – his – mal – thr – leu
- Hfr3: pro – leu – thr – mal
- Hfr4: lac – gal – his – mal – thr

**Step 2 — compute gene-to-gene intervals, not distance-from-origin.** This is the step that makes the tiling work. Entry times are measured from each strain's own oriT and are not comparable between strains, but the *gaps* are.

- Hfr1: thr–leu 12, leu–pro 18, pro–lac 15
- Hfr2: gal–his 10, his–mal 17, mal–thr 8, thr–leu 12
- Hfr3: pro–leu 18, leu–thr 12, thr–mal 8
- Hfr4: lac–gal 20, gal–his 10, his–mal 17, mal–thr 8

**Step 3 — spot the reversed strain.** Hfr1 and Hfr3 share three genes. Hfr1 reads thr–leu–pro; Hfr3 reads pro–leu–thr. Same three genes, same intervals (12 and 18), opposite direction. Hfr3 is not contradictory data — its F factor integrated in the opposite orientation, so its oriT fires the other way round the circle. **Flip Hfr3** to thr – leu – pro and it agrees with everything else.

Note you can only conclude Hfr3 is the odd one out because the other three mutually fix a shared orientation: Hfr1 and Hfr2 overlap on {thr, leu} in the same sense, and Hfr2 and Hfr4 overlap on {gal, his, mal, thr} in the same sense. Three strains agreeing outvote one.

**Step 4 — tile the strips on shared genes.**

- Hfr1 gives thr – leu – pro – lac.
- Hfr4 starts lac – gal – his – mal – thr, so it extends Hfr1 from lac onward: thr – leu – pro – lac – gal – his – mal – thr.
- That returns to thr, so the circle closes with seven genes.
- Hfr2 (gal – his – mal – thr – leu) and flipped Hfr3 (thr – leu – pro) both sit on this map without contradiction.

**Step 5 — assign distances,** reading them off the intervals from Step 2.

**Step 6 — check your work properly. This is the part students get wrong.** The tempting move is to add the intervals, get 100 minutes, and call that confirmation. **It is not.** Every arc was measured directly, so the total is just those same numbers added back up — you would get a self-consistent total no matter what the numbers were. Summing to 100 confirms nothing.

The real check is **cross-strain agreement on redundantly-measured arcs.** Five of the seven were measured by more than one strain, and all five agree exactly:

- thr–leu 12 — Hfr1, Hfr2, Hfr3
- leu–pro 18 — Hfr1, Hfr3
- gal–his 10 — Hfr2, Hfr4
- his–mal 17 — Hfr2, Hfr4
- mal–thr 8 — Hfr2, Hfr3, Hfr4

That is what tells you the tiling is right. Note the two arcs with **no** redundancy: **pro–lac (15), from Hfr1 alone**, and **lac–gal (20), from Hfr4 alone**. Those two rest on a single observation each — if this were real data you would want another Hfr crossing that region before trusting them.

**Step 7 — the dashes are information.** Every marker absent from a strain should fall beyond the 60-minute cutoff on your finished map. Check a couple: on Hfr1, gal lands at 70 and *mal* at 97, both past the cutoff. On Hfr3, *his* lands at 63 — only just past. If a missing marker came out *under* 60, your map is wrong.
rubric: Converted each strain's entry times into an ordered strip of genes
Computed gene-to-gene intervals rather than only distance from the origin of transfer
Identified Hfr3 as reversed and flipped it, rather than discarding it as bad data
Tiled the strips on their shared genes into one continuous closed circle
Got the order thr – leu – pro – lac – gal – his – mal (or its mirror image) with all seven intervals correct
Validated using cross-strain agreement on the redundant arcs, NOT by the intervals summing to 100
questions.src: L6 — the interrupted-mating map she guaranteed would be on the exam. Fresh dataset; the circular order was confirmed unique by exhaustive search over all 720 arrangements and both transfer directions.

questions.stem:
You run a classic dideoxy sequencing reaction on a cloned fragment and resolve the products on a denaturing polyacrylamide gel. Four lanes were loaded, one per dideoxynucleotide. The wells are at the top; the positive electrode is at the bottom. Bands are listed from the top of the gel to the bottom.

| Position on gel | ddA | ddC | ddG | ddT |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| top (slowest) | | | band | |
| | band | | | |
| | | band | | |
| | | | | band |
| | band | | | |
| | | | band | |
| | | | band | |
| | | band | | |
| | band | | | |
| bottom (fastest) | | | | band |

**(a)** Write out the sequence of the strand that was synthesized in the reaction, labelling its 5′ and 3′ ends.
**(b)** Write out the sequence of the template strand that was being read, labelling its 5′ and 3′ ends.
**(c)** State how you know which of your two answers is which, **and** how you know which end of each is 5′.
questions.answer:
**(a)** Synthesized strand: **5′-TACGGATCAG-3′**

**(b)** Template strand: **5′-CTGATCCGTA-3′**

**(c)** Two separate things have to be argued. **Which strand:** every band is a nascent chain terminated by an *incorporated* ddNTP, so the ladder is necessarily the polymerase's product — the template is never in the gel and has to be inferred. **Which end is 5′:** the shortest fragment terminated earliest, i.e. nearest the primer, so the bottom band is the **5′-most base of the new sequence**, and each step up the gel adds one base in the 5′→3′ direction.
questions.worked:
**Step 1 — orient the gel.** Smaller fragments migrate faster, so the **bottom** band is the shortest product, which means it terminated at the very first base added. Read the gel **bottom to top**.

**Step 2 — read the lanes from bottom to top.** The lane a band sits in names the ddNTP that stopped that fragment, which is the base incorporated into the **new** strand at that position:

T, A, C, G, G, A, T, C, A, G

**Step 3 — that sequence is the synthesized strand, 5′ to 3′.** Polymerase builds 5′→3′, and the first base added is the 5′-most base of the read:

**5′-TACGGATCAG-3′**

This is the answer to (a), and it is the one she warned people confuse — the gel does **not** show you the template.

**Step 4 — derive the template.** It is antiparallel and complementary. Line it up first:

`5′-T A C G G A T C A G-3′`  (synthesized)
`3′-A T G C C T A G T C-5′`  (template)

**Step 5 — rewrite the template 5′→3′,** which means reversing it. Do not just write the complement and stop — that is the classic error, and it gives ATGCCTAGTC, which is wrong:

**5′-CTGATCCGTA-3′**

**Why the ladder exists at all:** the reaction contains mostly normal dNTPs plus a small fraction of one ddNTP. The dideoxy sugar has no 3′-OH, so whenever one is incorporated that chain can form no further phosphodiester bond and terminates. Across millions of molecules this happens at every occurrence of that base, producing one fragment per position.

**A note on strictness:** each real fragment is 5′-[primer]-TACGGATCAG…-3′, and the template must extend past the region read, so these ten-mers are properly the *extension product* and the *template region read* rather than complete strands. Every textbook makes that idealization for a ten-band gel with no primer given, and the expected answer is unchanged.
rubric: Read the gel bottom to top, and justified it — the shortest fragment terminated earliest, so the bottom band is the 5′-most base of the new strand
Wrote the synthesized strand as 5′-TACGGATCAG-3′ with its ends labelled
Derived the template by reversing as well as complementing, not by complementing alone
Gave the template as 5′-CTGATCCGTA-3′ and said why the gel reports the synthesized strand (every band is a chain terminated by an incorporated ddNTP, so the template is never in the gel)
questions.src: L2 — she said there is a very good chance of a Sanger gel question, and explicitly flagged the synthesized-versus-template confusion as the trap.
